attachment.snippet:
1 # Recipe Bottle 2 3 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) provides two independent container image capabilities: 4 5 - **[Foundry](#Foundry)**: orchestrate Google Cloud Build to produce multiplatform container images (x86 + ARM), fetch, retain, and serve them using a role-managed private cloud registry — with optional [egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) builds and supply-chain [provenance](#Provenance) 6 - **[Crucible](#Crucible)**: run untrusted containers behind enforced network isolation — DNS filtering and IP filtering — even using images unmodified from "in the wild" 7 8 > [!IMPORTANT] 9 > **Early-stage project — security review welcome in both domains** 10 > 11 > The [Foundry's](#Foundry) egress-locked Cloud Build configuration — including the [SLSA](#Provenance) attestation chain, [build isolation](#BuildIsolation), and digest-pinned toolchains — has not yet had broad independent review. 12 > 13 > The [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime containment — a multi-container apparatus where the workload runs unprivileged in a network namespace it does not control — has also not had broad review, particularly the network isolation rules, privileged namespace setup, and egress enforcement. 14 > 15 > If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. 16 > Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued. 17 18 **Host platform scope.** [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is qualified for release-1 only on Linux and macOS with Docker. Windows host support is planned but not yet tested — see [Roadmap](#Roadmap) for the upcoming supply-chain and transport work that enables it. 19 20 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is a set of bash scripts enabling enterprise grade container image management intended for incorporation into any project. 21 The dependency footprint is deliberately narrow — `bash 3.2` and a handful of standard tools — with no Python runtime, no language-specific package manager, and no `gcloud` CLI. 22 After initial manual setup, all cloud API calls use `openssl` + `curl`. 23 A small team can stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without specialized DevOps expertise. 24 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) goal is a workflow where every container image has a verified origin and a controlled version, running behind appropriate network safeguards. 25 26 **Project page**: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle 27 28

29 30

31 32 ## Environment 33 34 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is organized around two independent capabilities: the [Foundry](#Foundry) builds container images with verifiable [provenance](#Provenance), and the [Crucible](#Crucible) runs untrusted images with enforced network isolation. 35 The two compose but neither requires the other. 36 37 ### Supported Platforms 38 39 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime is qualified for release-1 against **Docker** as the container runtime, on two host families: 40 41 - **Linux host** — native Docker Engine (Docker CE / `docker.io`) running directly on the host kernel; no VM. Tested on Ubuntu LTS with cgroup v2 and a 6.x kernel. 42 - **macOS host** — Docker Desktop for Mac on a supported macOS release. Apple Silicon hosts use the Apple Virtualization framework or Docker VMM hypervisor backend. 43 44 Windows host support is planned but not yet tested — see [Roadmap](#Roadmap). 45 Podman support is architecturally accommodated by the spec but deferred — see [Roadmap](#Roadmap). 46 47 All configuration flows through [Regimes](#Regime) — structured `.env` files with typed validation, each with its own render and validate commands. 48 Some regimes are committed in the repo: [Vessel](#Vessel) definitions ([RBRV](#RBRV)), [Nameplate](#Nameplate) configurations ([RBRN](#RBRN)), [Depot](#Depot) identity ([RBRD](#RBRD)), repository-wide settings ([RBRR](#RBRR)), and [Payor](#Payor) identity ([RBRP](#RBRP)). 49 Others live on the filesystem outside revision control: OAuth credentials ([RBRO](#RBRO)), role credentials ([RBRA](#RBRA)), and developer workstation paths ([BURS](#BURS)). 50 51 Every operation is launched through a [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) — a shell script in the `tt/` directory. 52 The critical property: tab completion finds the command you want. 53 Type `tt/rbw-` and the shell narrows to all [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) operations; type `tt/rbw-h` to see just the [Hallmark](#Hallmark) commands. 54 Each [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) is named `{colophon}.{frontispiece}.sh` — the colophon routes to the right module, the frontispiece tells you what it does. 55 56 Every state-changing [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) writes three [Log](#Log) files to the directory named by `BURS_LOG_DIR` in your [BURS](#BURS) station file: a stable-name file (always the same path — easy for tooling to locate and evaluate the most recent run), a per-command file (same command name across runs — tools like SlickEdit sense diffs between executions), and a timestamped historical file (permanent record). 57 Disk space is cheap; [Log](#Log) unconditionally so the diagnostic evidence is always there when something fails. 58 Handbooks don't [Log](#Log) — teaching output is ephemeral. 59 60 A [Transcript](#Transcript) is a single file capturing key decision points and state transitions within a [Tabtarget's](#Tabtarget) execution. 61 Where [Logs](#Log) preserve full terminal output, a [Transcript](#Transcript) records the structured progress of sophisticated orchestration commands — the first thing to read when debugging a multi-step failure. 62 63 The [Output](#Output) directory is a fixed-path staging area cleared and recreated before each [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) runs. 64 Commands that produce artifacts write them here. 65 Concurrent bash sessions share this path, so parallel commands can overwrite each other's [Output](#Output). 66 67 To begin, run the onboarding walkthrough: 68 69 ``` 70 tt/rbw-o.ONBOARDING.sh 71 ``` 72 73 ## Foundry 74 75 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) remote build orchestration system for producing, attesting, and distributing container images via Google Cloud Build and Google Artifact Registry. 76 The [Foundry](#Foundry) manages [Depot](#Depot) access, [Vessels](#Vessel) choreography, [Hallmark](#Hallmark) tracking, and build definitions. 77 Three [Vessel](#Vessel) modes determine how images enter the [Depot](#Depot): [Conjure](#Conjure) ([egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) build from source with [SLSA provenance](#Provenance)), [Bind](#Bind) (digest-pinned upstream mirror), and [Graft](#Graft) (local push). 78 Peer to [Crucible](#Crucible), which handles local runtime containment. 79 80 The [Foundry](#Foundry) orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce container images with [SLSA](#Provenance) attestation, software bills of material, reproducible multi-architecture builds, and digest-pinned toolchains — so every image has a verifiable origin story. 81 Builds run in an [egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) configuration, drawing from upstream base images mirrored into a project-owned [Depot](#Depot) registry — a fixed, self-contained supply chain independent of third-party registry availability. 82 83 ### Depot 84 85 The facility where container images are built and stored — has its own GCP project with an artifact registry and a storage bucket, funded under the [Manor's](#Manor) billing account. 86 The [Payor](#Payor) [Levies](#Levy) a [Depot](#Depot), and the [Governor](#Governor) administers access to it. 87 Each [Depot](#Depot) operates as an independent supply-chain boundary with its own credentials, builds, and registry. 88 89 Each [Depot](#Depot) supports two build egress profiles: 90 ... [517 lines truncated] ...

file.content:
**Two layers shared by all tools:**
- **BURC** (`.buk/burc.env`) — project structure: tabtarget dir, tools dir, temp/output dirs
- **BURS** (`../station-files/burs.env`) — developer machine: log directory. Not in git.

**Recipe Bottle regimes** (in `.rbk/`):
- **RBRP** — Payor project identity. Set `RBRP_PAYOR_PROJECT_ID` to your GCP project.
- **RBRR** — Repository/depot configuration. Region, machine type, vessel directory, secrets directory, depot project ID.
- **RBRN** — Nameplate. Per-vessel: runtime (`docker`), vessel names, hallmark values (set after builds complete).
- **RBRV** — Vessel definitions. One per container image you want to build.
- **RBRS** — Station. Developer-specific paths for Recipe Bottle. Not in git.

## Architecture

```
Project Root/
├── .buk/                    # BUK launcher directory
│   ├── burc.env             # Project structure config
│   └── launcher.*.sh        # Launcher scripts (environment gates)
├── .rbk/                    # Recipe Bottle config regimes
│   ├── rbrp.env             # Payor regime
│   ├── rbrr.env             # Repo/depot regime
│   └── {moniker}/rbrn.env   # Nameplate regimes (per vessel)
├── tt/                      # TabTargets (ls this to see all commands)
├── Tools/
│   ├── buk/                 # Bash Utility Kit (portable infrastructure)
│   └── rbk/                 # Recipe Bottle Kit (domain logic)
└── rbev-vessels/            # Vessel definitions (rbrv.env + optional Dockerfile per vessel)
```

content.text:
Clean state — only the two markdown files are pending, and `jjx_close` will commit exactly those.

## Pre-wrap status for ₢BKAAH

**Done:**
- Spec surface (notched `e650b27`): `rbsld_moorings` established as a single full MCM quoin; each regime file's location consolidated into its existing regime definition; prose references the regime concept. The literal `rbmm_moorings` now appears **exactly once** in the assembled spec.
- Markdown surface (pending wrap): README.md + CLAUDE.consumer.md directory displays reworked to the single `rbmm_moorings/` umbrella; inline paths updated.
- Wrap criterion 1 (no `.buk`/`.rbk`/`rbev-vessels` across `vov_veiled/`, `rbh0/`, README, CLAUDE) — **clean**. Handbook tracks (`rbh0/`) needed no changes (earlier literal sweep covered them).

**Three honest loose ends — none blocking, your call whether any becomes an itch:**

1. **Naked filenames remain in operational prose.** `rbrv.env` (and a few `rbrr.env`) still appear bare in ~15 operational sentences (RBSDY, RBSAV, RBSAE, RBSAG, RBSDI…) — e.g., "source vessel configuration from `rbrv.env`." These aren't legacy-path vocabulary (out of this pace's scope) and we deliberately chose not to mint per-file quoins. Your "no naked filename" principle, applied fully, would sweep these too — a separate effort.

2. **Pre-existing broken include.** RBS0:1525 `include::RBSPV-PodmanVmSupplyChain.adoc[]` points at a top-level path, but the file lives only in `FUTURE/`. So RBSPV isn't assembled (I left it literal). Not mine to fix in a doc-vocabulary pace, but worth knowing.

3. **`rbsld_moorings` annotation.** I voiced `//axl_voices axtu_path` — the path motif fits, but I omitted a "kind" motif (`axrg_variable`/`axrd_constructed` didn't apply to a fixed root). A maintainer may want to refine it.

**Couldn't run:** the "AsciiDoc linked terms resolve" criterion (no asciidoctor locally) — validated by reasoning + the substitution-semantics web check + matching the existing quoin pattern this spec already renders.

Ready to wrap ₢BKAAH whenever you confirm.

message.content:
1) We did discover some regime file quoins though: would those be more appropriate than the naked filenames you found?

2) Don't worry overmuch about this file, it is cruft and I tolerate that>

3) Okay as is.

content.text:
Good question — and yes, for *most* of them the regime concept quoin is more appropriate, by the same principle we just applied. But it's not a blanket substitution; the sites split into two kinds:

**Conceptual / locating mentions — `{rbXX_regime}` is clearly better:**
- RBSAV / RBSAG: "Source vessel configuration from `rbrv.env`" → "…from the {rbrv_regime}"
- RBSDU:36: "in `rbrr.env`, or run marshal-zero" → "in the {rbrr_regime} file"
- RBSRV:202: "scanning … for rbrv.env files" → "for {rbrv_regime} files"
- RBS0:2020: "A vessel's regime file (`rbrv.env`)" → already says "regime file" — the gloss becomes redundant with `{rbrv_regime}`

**File-mechanical procedural steps — naked filename is arguably *better*:**
- RBSDY (yoke): "Load the vessel's `rbrv.env` into memory, line by line" / "atomically replace `rbrv.env` via rename." These describe byte-level file operations in a procedural register. A concept link (`{rbrv_regime}`) *abstracts away* the concrete file exactly where the prose is about manipulating it as a file. Here the literal reads truer.

So it's a per-site judgment call across ~15 sites, and — importantly — it's a **different concern** from the moorings cutover (these were never legacy paths; it's quoin-hygiene consistency). I'd resist expanding ₢BKAAH to absorb it, since the pace's contract was the `.buk`/`.rbk`/`rbev-vessels` vocabulary and that's now clean.

My recommendation: **capture it as an itch** — "regime-file naked-filename → concept-quoin sweep (conceptual sites only, leave file-mechanical prose literal)." Then wrap ₢BKAAH as-is.

Want me to (a) record that itch and wrap, (b) do the conceptual-site sweep now inside this pace, or (c) just wrap and leave it?

message.content: I urge you to replace all naked filenames.  I understand your reticence, but it is cleaner that way.  Someday we'll concretize rbXX_regime to be a concrete file, but not today, we need to wind this pace down.  Make this the last change and notch.

content.text: Understood — replace them all, leave the eventual concretization of `rbXX_regime` for another day. The four regime-definition location lines stay (they're the authoritative home of each basename — they can't reference themselves), but every operational mention becomes the concept quoin. Let me get the complete inventory:

RBS0-SpecTop.adoc:2919:File-sourced singleton at {rbsld_moorings}rbrd.env.

FUTURE/RBSPV-PodmanVmSupplyChain.adoc:100:These variables are defined in the RBRM (Machine) regime at `rbmm_moorings/rbrm.env`, separated from the broader RBRR configuration to clarify the distinction between Recipe Bottle's application-level configuration and the underlying VM infrastructure configuration.
=== rbrn.env ===
RBS0-SpecTop.adoc:3430:File-sourced, one per nameplate: an rbrn.env in each nameplate subdirectory under {rbsld_moorings}, the subdirectory named by the nameplate's {rbrn_moniker}.

RBSDY-director_yoke.adoc:4:NOTE: This operation writes `RBRV_RELIQUARY=«STAMP»` into every vessel's `rbrv.env` under `${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}` so that subsequent Cloud Build operations (conjure, bind, graft, enshrine) consume the named tool image set as GCB step images.
RBSDY-director_yoke.adoc:9:This primitive does not commit; the caller (orchestrator or operator) commits the rbrv.env changes.

RBSDY-director_yoke.adoc:67:. *Rewrite rbrv.env* (per vessel):
RBSDY-director_yoke.adoc:68:.. Load the vessel's `rbrv.env` into memory, line by line
RBSDY-director_yoke.adoc:72:.. Write the composed content to a per-vessel temp file, then atomically replace `rbrv.env` via rename
RBSDY-director_yoke.adoc:77:Successful execution writes `RBRV_RELIQUARY=«STAMP»` into the `rbrv.env` of every vessel discovered under `${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}`; no GAR state is modified.

content.content:
=== RBSDY 58-78 ===
//axhos_step
. *Iterate Vessels under `${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}`*:
.. Enumerate every immediate subdirectory of `${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}` containing an `rbrv.env` file
.. {rbbc_require}
at least one vessel found — empty enumeration is a fatal error (nothing to yoke)
.. For each enumerated vessel, perform *Rewrite rbrv.env* below in directory order
.. Maintain a running list of vessels successfully written; emit per-vessel progress

//axhos_step
. *Rewrite rbrv.env* (per vessel):
.. Load the vessel's `rbrv.env` into memory, line by line
.. For each line matching `^RBRV_RELIQUARY=` — substitute with `RBRV_RELIQUARY=«STAMP»`, preserving position
.. All other lines pass through unchanged
.. If no `RBRV_RELIQUARY=` line was found — append one under a `# Tool Image Reliquary` comment header
.. Write the composed content to a per-vessel temp file, then atomically replace `rbrv.env` via rename
.. {rbbc_fatal}
on any per-vessel I/O failure — the error message names the failing vessel and the list of vessels already written, so the operator can resume from a known point

//axhoc_completion
Successful execution writes `RBRV_RELIQUARY=«STAMP»` into the `rbrv.env` of every vessel discovered under `${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}`; no GAR state is modified.
The primitive does not commit — the caller (orchestrator or operator) commits the modified `rbrv.env` files with their usual git workflow.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
//axhob_operation
{rbtgo_payor_refresh}

Manual procedure to install OAuth credentials for new setup or refresh expired/compromised credentials.
Use this for both initial credential installation after
{rbtgo_payor_establish}
and for renewal operations.

//axhos_step
. *Obtain OAuth Credentials*:
.. For initial setup: Use JSON file downloaded during
{rbtgo_payor_establish}
.. For refresh/renewal: Navigate to APIs & Services > Credentials in Payor Project
... Click existing "Recipe Bottle Payor" OAuth client to open details
... If secret compromised:
.... Click "+ Add secret"
.... Click download icon next to NEW secret
.... Browser downloads `client_secret_[id].apps.googleusercontent.com.json`
.... Click "Disable" on secret with older creation date
.... Click trash icon to delete that disabled secret

//axhos_step
. *Install/Refresh Credentials*:
.. Run: `rbgp_payor_install ~/Downloads/client_secret_*.json`
.. This will:
... Guide you through OAuth authorization flow
... Store secure credentials in `~/.rbw/payor/rbro.env`
... Test the authentication
... Initialize depot tracking
... Update `RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID` in `rbrp.env`

//axhog_guarantee
* `rbgp_depot_list` successfully displays current depots without authentication errors

//axhoc_completion
Procedure is complete when `rbgp_depot_list` successfully displays current depots without authentication errors.

NOTE: OAuth refresh tokens in testing mode expire after 6 months of non-use.

lines: — after the depot-tracking refresh, using the Payor OAuth access token already in scope.

A pre-push existence guard (`docker manifest inspect`) makes inscription idempotent-by-refusal: an already-present tripwire is fatal, since RBRD is frozen for the depot's productive lifetime and refreshing it requires a fresh depot (unmake + relevy).

-The tripwire is _consumed_ inline by every cloud-submitting command before it dispatches a build: the command pulls the image, extracts `/rbrd.env`, and exact-byte-compares it (comments, whitespace, and line ordering all participate) against the local {rbrd_regime} assignment file.

+The tripwire is _consumed_ inline by every cloud-submitting command before it dispatches a build: the command pulls the image, extracts the embedded {rbrd_regime} assignment file, and exact-byte-compares it (comments, whitespace, and line ordering all participate) against the local copy.
A mismatch, a missing image, or a registry/auth failure is fatal with recovery guidance — the command refuses to submit cloud work against a drifted or unverifiable depot regime.
The check takes a bearer token parameter and is role-agnostic; each consuming command passes its own role's token (no new IAM grants).

Notched as `8ab31a79`. ₢BKAAH is now functionally complete.

## Final state of ₢BKAAH

**Three commits this session:**
- `e650b27` — spec-surface moorings cutover via MCM consolidation (`rbsld_moorings` quoin + regime-definition location lines)
- `8ab31a79` — markdown umbrella rework + naked-filename elimination

**What landed:**
- The literal `rbmm_moorings` appears **once** in the assembled spec (the `rbsld_moorings` quoin reftext).
- Every regime filename basename now survives only in its **six authoritative definition location lines** (rbrr/rbrd/rbrm/rbrp at moorings root, rbrv under `{rbrr_vessel_dir}` by sigil, rbrn under nameplate subdir by moniker). All operational prose references the regime concept quoin.
- README.md + CLAUDE.consumer.md directory displays collapsed to the single `rbmm_moorings/` umbrella.
- Wrap criterion 1 (no `.buk`/`.rbk`/`rbev-vessels` across pace scope) — clean.

**Deferred / acknowledged:**
- Concretizing `rbXX_regime` quoins to render as the file path itself — your "someday, not today."
- FUTURE/RBSPV — unassembled cruft, left literal (you're tolerating it).
- `rbsld_moorings` annotation voices `axtu_path` only (no kind-motif) — fine as-is per your call.

I have **not** wrapped — that's your call. Ready to `jjx_close` ₢BKAAH whenever you confirm, with a summary along the lines of the two commit intents.
